content.problemStatement:
Now Chef is very good in cooking; his dishes are the best in the city, so he wants to make his restaurant prettier. The first, what customer sees, when comes to his restaurant is the door. So he will start with it. He has found a new design last night. Hi will cut out some circles from the door and fill the holes by the glass to make windows of peculiar form.

Formally, we can model the process Chef will perform to create the windows as follows. Let the door be a rectangle at the coordinate plane with corners at the points **(0, 0)**, **(W, 0)**, **(0, H)**, **(W, H)**. Initially the door is colored white, while the remaining part of the coordinate plane is colored black. At each step Chef colors in black some circle including all its inner points. After Chef will color all required circles, the black part of the door (that is, black regions that lie in the rectangle corresponding to the door) is cut out and replaced by the glass.

The main measure of peculiarity of the door is the perimeter of all the windows considering only those parts of windows borders lying inside the door (see example case 2 for clarity). Chef is very tired after coloring so many circles and asks you to find this number.
constraints: The sum of all values of N over the input does not exceed 5000
X, Y, R have exactly two digits after the decimal point
All circles are different, that is, every two circles have either different radii or different centers (or both)
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains three space-separated integers W, H, and N, denoting the width and height of the door and the number of circles. The next N lines contain three space-separated real numbers X, Y, and R, denoting the center coordinates and radius of each circle.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the sum of the perimeters of all the windows as described in the problem statement, rounded to six decimal places.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The first test case has 8 circles, and the second test case has 3 circles. The output is the sum of the perimeters of all the circles that intersect with the door.
The circle intersects with all four edges of the door.
The circle intersects with the top-left corner of the door.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we need to calculate the perimeter of each circle that intersects with the door. We can do this by checking if the circle intersects with the edges of the door and calculating the length of the arc that lies within the door. We can use the formula for the circumference of a circle and the properties of circle-rectangle intersection to calculate the perimeter.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N) per test case, O(T * N) total. No precomputation is done.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — the space used does not grow with the size of the input.
